Yu, Hesheng
coredata.dc:description: This work focuses on modifying BiOCl using graphene quantum dots (GQDs) via a semi-solvothermal method. The formed composites (GQDs/BOC) nanomaterials have enhanced catalytic activity in NO photocatalysis. Multiple characterizations are performed on the prepared GQDs/BOC to describe their crystallization, morphology, and photoelectric characteristics. In addition, photocatalyst activity of GQDs/BOC nanocomposites are assessed by treating 30–32 ppm of NO under visible-light illumination. Within 1 h of photocatalytic NO degradation, GQDs30/BOC behaves the best. Its conversion rate of NO reaches 81.42 % and the DeNOx index is 0.55; they are 1.81 and 2.20 times higher than the counterparts of BiOCl, respectively. The addition of GQDs increases the specific surface area of the BiOCl catalyst, improves the absorption of visible light, accelerates photogenerated carrier transport and inhibits their recombination. These roles of GQDs improve photocatalytic performance. The analysis of the degradation of NO by GQDs/BOC is explored experimentally and theoretically. GQDs in the GQDs/BOC composites are confirmed to act as electron acceptors. In addition, GQDs30/BOC retains excellent catalytic activity after four cycles of testing, evidencing good stability.

head.ce:title: Graphene quantum dots/BiOCl visible-light active photocatalyst for degradation of NO

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This work focuses on modifying BiOCl using graphene quantum dots (GQDs) via a semi-solvothermal method. The formed composites (GQDs/BOC) nanomaterials have enhanced catalytic activity in NO photocatalysis. Multiple characterizations are performed on the prepared GQDs/BOC to describe their crystallization, morphology, and photoelectric characteristics. In addition, photocatalyst activity of GQDs/BOC nanocomposites are assessed by treating 30–32 ppm of NO under visible-light illumination. Within 1 h of photocatalytic NO degradation, GQDs30/BOC behaves the best. Its conversion rate of NO reaches 81.42 % and the DeNOx index is 0.55; they are 1.81 and 2.20 times higher than the counterparts of BiOCl, respectively. The addition of GQDs increases the specific surface area of the BiOCl catalyst, improves the absorption of visible light, accelerates photogenerated carrier transport and inhibits their recombination. These roles of GQDs improve photocatalytic performance. The analysis of the degradation of NO by GQDs/BOC is explored experimentally and theoretically. GQDs in the GQDs/BOC composites are confirmed to act as electron acceptors. In addition, GQDs30/BOC retains excellent catalytic activity after four cycles of testing, evidencing good stability.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: For environmental and public health reasons, nitrogen oxides (NOx) in the atmosphere should be effectively controlled (Chang et al., 2022; Zhu et al., 2021). The main composition of NOx is NO, which accounts for 90–95 % of NOx (Li et al., 2023). Therefore, the core of reducing the hazard of NOx is to effectively control NO. Traditional methods for removing NO mainly include selective catalytic reduction (SCR) (Jing et al., 2022), selective non-catalytic reduction (SNCR) (Yang et al., 2020), and ternary catalysis (Li et al., 2021). However, wide deployment of these technologies is still limited because of their high operating cost, complex equipment, and secondary pollution. A cost-effective method is, therefore, demanded to degrade NO. Recent advances in photocatalysis have demonstrated its ability to efficiently and sustainably address NO (Huang et al., 2022; Zhang et al., 2019).

Among the recently reported photocatalysts, bismuth chloride oxide (BiOCl) and its composites stand out as particularly appealing options for NO removal due to special hierarchical crystal structure with remarkably catalytic activity (Lee et al., 2017). The BiOCl structure is made up of overlapping sheets of [Bi2O2] and layers of [Cl]. During the formation of [Bi2O2] sheets, Bi and O atoms are interconnected by strong covalent bonds. As contrast, the van der Waals force between a sheet of [Bi2O2] and a layer of [Cl] is relatively weak. The difference in strain between the layers leads to the formation of an electric field inside BiOCl, which promotes the separation of photogenerated carriers (Sun et al., 2018; Wu et al., 2019). BiOCl has a weak absorption of visible light and a limited number of active sites (Cao et al., 2021; Niu et al., 2020), despite the unique layered structure of pure BiOCl. In recent studies, defect engineering (Zhang et al., 2017), exposing facets (Li et al., 2017), and material coupling (Jiang et al., 2014) have been proposed to improve the response of BiOCl to visible-light. Among them, material coupling is proven effective for enhancing the photocatalytic performance of BiOCl.

Material coupling includes doping with nonmetal elements (Zhang and Antonietti, 2010), modification by noble metals (Xue et al., 2015), and semiconductor coupling (Zhang et al., 2021). Recently, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have been hybridized with other photocatalysts to enhance catalytic activity due to their distinct photochemical and electrochemical properties (Mahvelati-Shamsabadi et al., 2018; Niazi et al., 2021). For example, N-GQDs loaded onto BiOBr enhances the degradation efficiency of RhB (Yin et al., 2016), because the formed N-GQDs/BiOBr nanocomposites have high electrical conductivity and good light harvesting capability. Zhao et al. (2021) also prepared GQDs/BiOCl heterojunction by combining GQDs with BiOCl via hydrothermal method, and the toluene degradation efficiency of toluene increased from 32.1 % to 65.4 %. In GQDs/BiOCl, photogenerated electrons can be captured and rapidly transferred to produce superoxide radicals, which promote the degradation of toluene (2021). Therefore, GQDs/BiOCl composite is intuitively expected to degrade NO effectively. However, to our best knowledge, such research has not been reported. Furthermore, the short-term exposure limit for NO is established at 25 ppm (Çağlayan et al., 2020), but most prior research efforts have primarily addressed the removal of NO at ppb levels. Our study breaks new ground by not only introducing GQDs/BiOCl for the novel purpose of NO removal but also addressing NO concentrations at 30–32 ppm. This new approach holds valuable implications for the practical applications of photocatalytic technology in mitigating real-world NO pollutants.

Herein, GQDs/BiOCl nanocomposites are prepared using a semi-solvothermal (water-ethylene glycol) approach. Then the samples are characterized in terms of crystal phase, morphology, optical properties, bond structure, and photoelectric properties by multiple technologies. After that, by degrading NO under visible-light illumination, BiOCl and GQDs/BiOCl hybrids are compared for their photocatalytic activities. Finally, the mechanisms behind NO photocatalysis are systematically explored based on active species detection test, Mott-Schottky (MS), electron spin resonance (ESR), and density functional theory (DFT) calculation.

Reagents include bismuth nitrate pentahydrate (Bi(NO3)3·5H2O), ethanol, ethylene glycol (EG), sodium sulfate (Na2SO4), sodium chloride (NaCl), sodium hydroxide (NaOH), and citric acid (CA). All are bought from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (China). Except for Bi(NO3)3·5H2O and NaOH of analytical purity, all other reagents are of guaranteed purity. Additionally, Direct-Q 5 UV (Merck Millipore, Germany) produces ultrapure water having 18.2 MΩ·cm−1 resistivity.

GQDs is prepared according to the method reported in Reference (Cui et al., 2021). 2 g of CA are pyrolyzed in a round bottom flask with a capacity of 25 ml at 200 °C for 30 min. Afterwards, the pH of solution is regulated to 7 by 0.25 M NaOH solution. A pH meter (S470-K, Mettler-Toledo, USA) is used to monitor the pH of solution. To further characterize GQDs, we dialyzed the resulting solution in a dialysis bag with a molecular weight cutoff of 3500 Da for 3–4 days and removed the solvent through freeze-drying (Goharshadi et al., 2017).

GQDs/BiOCl nanocomposites are prepared using a semi-solvothermal method as follows. First, 4.32 mmol Bi(NO3)3·5H2O and 4.32 mmol NaCl are mixed in 36 ml of EG, and agitated for 30 min to form Solution A. Then 10–40 ml GQDs solution is added to Solution A; the volume of this mixture is fixed at 76 ml with ultrapure water if needed. Afterwards, the mixture is stirred continuously for 1 h to obtain precursor solvent. After stirring, the precursor solvent is transferred into a 100 ml Teflon-lined autoclave, followed by heating at 140 °C in an oven (Tuohao, DHG-9140A, China) for 12 h. Then the autoclave naturally cools to room temperature, and the solids are gained through centrifugation with 4500 rpm, before cleaning with ultrapure water and ethanol individually. The washing is repeated three times. The wet solids are then heated in a 60 °C oven for 12 h. The collected samples are denoted as GQDs10/BOC, GQDs20/BOC, GQDs30/BOC, and GQDs40/BOC, corresponding to the GQDs dosages of 10, 20, 30, and 40 ml, respectively. Furthermore, pristine BiOCl samples are prepared following the procure reported in Reference (Yin et al., 2016) for comparison.

Given space restraint, the detailed information about sample characterizations, the evaluation of photocatalytic activity, photoelectrical measurements and DFT calculations are presented in detail in the Supporting Information (SI).

The catalysts are evaluated in terms of NO removal under visible-light irradiation at room temperature and atmospheric pressure. Fig. S1 presents the schematic of the testing rig for photocatalytic degradation of NO within a rectangular ISO reactor with the volume of 25 cm3 (10 × 5 × 0.5 cm3). The 300 W xenon lamp (Kajing, KJS300W, China) with a 380 nm UV filter is placed 15 cm above the reactor to provide visible lights. The light intensity measured on the surface of catalyst using a radiometer (FZ-A, Beijing Normal University, China) is 68.85 mW/cm2. Simulated gas streams with a NO concentration of 30–32 ppm and an oxygen content of 5 % are prepared by regulating the flow rates of NO (1001 ppm with N2 balance), high-purity oxygen and nitrogen with mass flow controllers (CS200A, Zhengzhou Ketan, China). The flow rate of the simulated gas stream is maintained at 200 ml/min. During the tests, relative humidity measured by a hygrometer (UNI-T, UT333, China) is 55 % ± 5%. The concentrations of NO, NO2, and gaseous HNO2 at the reactor outlet are measured and recorded every 15 s using a continuous FTIR analyzer (MKS MultiGas 2060, USA).

The procedure for a typical NO-degradation test is as follows. First, 80 mg of catalysts is evenly coated on a glass sheet (5 × 10 cm2), which is then placed into the ISO reactor. The gas mixture flows throughout the reactor in the dark for 30 min to reach dynamic equilibrium between adsorption and desorption. Then, the outlet NO concentration remains stable for 10 min before the Xenon lamp is activated for 1 h for photocatalytic test.

Fig. 1 a illustrates XRD spectra for BiOCl and GQDs/BOC samples. The positions of all diffraction peaks match the BiOCl crystal standard card (PDF# 06–0249) (Shamaila et al., 2011). The three primary characteristic peaks located at 25.86°, 32.50°, 33.45° in the spectra belong to (101), (110), and (102) crystal planes of BiOCl, respectively. Furthermore, the intense and sharp peaks show that the prepared catalysts possess great crystallinity. The characteristic peaks for GQDs in the composites are not detectable due to their low content and their uniform distribution on the BiOCl surface. Similar findings are also reported for other GQDs-based composites (Pan et al., 2015; Tian et al., 2015). However, the intensity of the peak of (101) plane becomes weaker, whereas that of (110) plane becomes stronger with increasing dosage of GQDs. This is because the sodium citrate in the GQDs can increase the relative growth rate and the exposure of (110) crystal plane (Ma et al., 2022). Moreover, no other peaks are observed, demonstrating excellent purity of synthesized composite catalysts. Fig. S3 shows the XRD spectrum of GQDs, the peak located at 32° corresponding to the (002) surface of GQDs. The peak is wider due to the smaller size of GQDs (Goharshadi et al., 2017).

Fig. 1b–f present the XPS results of BiOCl and GQDs30/BOC. All spectra are corrected against C 1s at 284.8 eV. Fig. 1b demonstrates that C, O, Bi and Cl elements are observed in full spectrum scan of XPS. Fig. 1c illustrates C 1s XPS spectrum obtained by multimodal fitting. The three peaks located at 284.8 eV, 286.3 eV, and 288.0 eV for BiOCl (284.8 eV, 286.4 eV, and 288.3 eV for GQDs30/BOC) correspond to C-C, C-O, and C=O groups, respectively; they were observed in both BiOCl and GQDs30/BOC (Wu et al., 2023). Fig. 1d demonstrates the high-resolution spectra of O 1s. The binding energies at 530.5 eV and 531.9 eV for BiOCl (530.0 eV and 532.1 eV for GQDs30/BOC) correspond to the lattice oxygen atoms and adsorbed oxygen, respectively (Wu et al., 2023). Moreover, the binding energy of 531.0 eV in the GQDs30/BOC spectrum is assigned to the O-C/O-H bonds from GQDs (Deng et al., 2017). In Fig. 1e, the binding energies at 197.9 eV for pure BiOCl (198.0 eV for GQDs30/BOC) correspond to Cl 2p3/2, and that at 199.5 eV for pure BiOCl (199.6 eV for GQDs30/BOC) correspond to Cl 2p1/2; they are originated from the Cl− in BiOCl (Long et al., 2022). Furthermore, Fig. 1f demonstrates two binding energies of 159.2 eV (159.3 eV for GQDs30/BOC) and 164.5 eV (164.6 eV for GQDs30/BOC), which are attributed to Bi 4f7/2 and Bi 4f5/2 spin states, respectively. It means that the valence of Bi element in both pure and composite catalysts remains +3 (Wang et al., 2022). According to the C 1s, O 1s, Cl 2p, and Bi 4f spectra, introducing GQDs causes a shift in the peak location. Moreover, the O 1 s spectrum indicates that GQDs is successfully loaded onto the BiOCl samples.

Table S1 shows the proportions of each element in the XPS test results. For BiOCl, the molar ratio of Bi element to Cl element is approximately 1:1, which is consistent with the proportion of elements in BiOCl. For GQDs30/BWO, the proportion of Cl element decreases, which may be due to the introduction of GQDs reducing the exposure of Cl element on the catalyst surface.

Fig. 2 shows the morphology of BiOCl and GQDs30/BOC samples determined by SEM and HR-TEM. The BiOCl are regular nanoflakes and have a size around 100–200 nm (see Fig. 2a). The GQDs30/BOC composites, shown in Fig. 2b, are block structures consisting of nanosheets with size of around 50 nm, much smaller than that of BiOCl. Reduced particle size enhances NO removal efficiency by increasing the probability of NO molecules to contact with catalyst. Fig. 2c–f show the EDS elemental mapping of GQD30/BOC. The elements O, Bi and Cl are uniformly spaced over the catalyst, and C elements, which originate from GQDs, are also observed. The homogeneous GQDs distributed over BiOCl surface is confirmed. Table S2 shows the mass ratios of each element in EDS test results. Compared to the molar ratio of Bi element to Cl element in BiOCl being 1, the proportion of Cl element in GQD30/BOC decreases, which is consistent with the XPS results.

Fig. 2g shows the HR-TEM images of BiOCl nanomaterials. Clear and bright interphase lattice stripes with a spacing of 0.34 nm can be observed in the figure, which corresponds to (101) crystal plane of BiOCl (Radon et al., 2020). However, after adding GQDs, the lattice spacing of BiOCl changes to 0.27 nm, corresponding to its (110) crystal plane (Ma et al., 2022) (see Fig. 2h); the change in major exposed facet before and after adding GQDs is in accordance with the XRD findings in Fig. 1a. Fig. 2i indicates that the size of the prepared GQDs is approximately 5 nm, falling within the size range of quantum dots. Meanwhile, GQDs could be observed with a lattice spacing of 0.21 nm in Fig. 2h and j (Yu et al., 2016b). This again evidences the existence of GQDs in the prepared GQD30/BOC composites.

In addition, Fig. S4 shows adsorption–desorption curves of N2 for BiOCl and GQDs30/BOC. It can be found that these isotherms are type IV, indicating that the pore structure in the catalyst is mesoporous (Islam et al., 2011). These two photocatalysts have pore widths between 5 and 60 nm. However, GQDs30/BOC has a larger average pore size of 29.71 nm than BiOCl, which has a pore size of 13.08 nm. The specific surface area of GQDs30/BOC is 34.59 m2/g, greater than 5.16 m2/g for BiOCl. According to the results, GQDs can significantly increase the pore size and specific surface area of catalysts, improve the contact probability between catalyst and NO, and boost the conversion efficiency of NO (Wang et al., 2020). This finding concurs with the changes of particle sizes observed in the SEM images (see Fig. 2a and b).

The UV–Vis DRS tests examine the photo-absorption of samples. Fig. 3 a shows that all catalysts respond to visible lights (>380 nm). Fig. S5 shows that the GQDs have a strong absorption in the UVA region. The presence of GQDs favors the catalyst’s adsorption of light, which is beneficial to photocatalytic degradation of NO. There are two possible reasons for increased visible-light harvesting. First, as a photosensitive material, GQDs can facilitate the capture of visible light (Liu et al., 2017). Second, the addition of GQDs changes the color of catalyst from gray to black, thus improving the absorption of light (Liu et al., 2021). And Fig. 3a shows that GQDs40/BOC exhibits the highest absorption in the ultraviolet region, which is due to the light down-conversion effect of GQDs (Zhuo et al., 2012), enhancing the material's ultraviolet absorption. However, within the visible light range, the introduction of an appropriate amount of GQDs can achieve the best synergy between GQDs and BiOCl, resulting in the strongest absorption in the visible light region. Excessive introduction of GQDs can disrupt this balance, leading to a wider bandgap of GQDs40/BOC compared to GQDs30/BOC. Fig. 3b shows band gaps of all samples, which are calculated by the formula:
( α h v )
1 / n
= A
( h v - E g )
, where α, h, ν, Eg and A are the absorption coefficient, Planck constant, light frequency, band gap, and a constant, respectively (Lin et al., 2023). In GQDs/BOC system, n is 2 because BiOCl has indirect band gaps (Yu et al., 2016a). Then, the band gaps (Eg) of BiOCl, GQDs10/BOC, GQDs20/BOC, GQDs30/BOC, and GQDs40/BOC are graphically determined as 3.19, 3.09, 2.96, 2.54 and 2.75 eV, respectively. These findings suggest that the inclusion of GQDs narrows the band gap of BiOCl and enhances the capability of light absorption(Guo et al., 2021). Furthermore, the inset within Fig. S5 demonstrates that the prepared GQDs have a non-zero bandgap.

The potential at the edge of the conduction band (CB) is calculated via the flat band potential (Efb ) determined by MS measurements. Fig. 3c illustrates the MS curves for BiOCl and GQDs30/BOC. These samples are n-type semiconductors because their MS curves have a positive slope (Hu et al., 2018). The Efb of BiOCl and GQDs30/BOC are −0.91 V and −0.78 V vs. SCE. Based on E(NHE) = E(SCE) + 0.24 V (Zhou et al., 2021), they are −0.67 V and −0.54 V vs. NHE. It is known that CB potential for n-type semiconductor is about −0.1 V negative than Efb potential (Zhu et al., 2022). Therefore, CB potentials of BiOCl and GQDs30/BOC are −0.77 V and −0.64 V vs. NHE. Finally, based on EVB = Eg + ECB , the calculated potentials of valence band (VB) of BiOCl and GQDs30/BOC are 2.42 V and 1.90 V vs. NHE.

Fig. 3d illustrates the PL spectra of all the prepared samples. When excited by a light source at 320 nm, all spectra have similar shapes and their primary peaks are located at 468 nm. The PL spectrum characterizes the recombination of photo-generated electron and hole pairs, and stronger intensity of PL means higher recombination rate. Therefore, photocatalysts with smaller excitation peak usually have lower carrier recombination rates, and thus exhibit better catalytic activity (Reddy et al., 2020). GQDs30/BOC exhibits the weakest PL intensity, signifying its lowest efficiency of recombination between photogenerated electrons (e −) and hole (h+ ). The PL intensity of GQDs10/BOC and GQDs20/BOC is weaker than that of BiOCl, indicating that the presence of GQDs prohibits electron-hole recombination. Interestingly, the PL intensity of GQDs40/BOC is stronger than that of BiOCl. This may be due to the excessive introduction of GQDs, which enhances the separation and quenching of photogenerated e − and h+ , resulting in GQDs40/BOC having the most efficient e −- h+ recombination. These results prove the importance of introducing an appropriate amount of GQDs in enhancing the electrochemical properties of composite materials.

EIS provides a useful tool for assessing the catalytic activity in terms of carrier separation efficiency. The smaller diameter of EIS Nyquist curve arc, the faster separation of photogenerated carriers (Liu et al., 2022). Fig. 3e shows that the diameter of the Nyquist diagram for GQDs30/BOC is smaller than that of BiOCl. The incorporation of GQDs boosts transport of photogenerated carriers at the interface between GQDs and BiOCl, thus favoring the enhancement of photocatalytic activity.

Photocurrent measurement is widely regarded as another typical method to study electron-hole separation efficiency in semiconductor materials. Fig. 3f shows that photocurrents of all GQDs/BOC samples are higher than that of BiOCl. Greater photocurrent implies faster separation rate of photogenerated carriers (Zhou et al., 2020). These results further confirms that GQDs facilitate carrier separation, which agrees with the findings of the EIS.

Fig. S6a shows that the instantaneous efficiency of NO degradation of BiOCl gradually drops from 82.80 % to 29.32 % within 1 h. However, that of GQDs30/BOC slightly decreases from 98.01 % to 70.43 %. Such finding shows that the presence of GQDs improves the efficiency of the photocatalyst in treating NO and inhibits the deactivation of the photocatalyst. Fig. S6b–d show the time-series concentrations of NO, NO2 and gaseous HNO2 within 1 h, which are then utilized to calculate the η (see Fig. 4 a) and DeNOx index (see Fig. 4b) according to Eqs. S1 and S2.

Fig. 4a presents the photocatalytic conversion efficiency for NO using various catalysts. As GQDs amount increases, the degradation efficiency for NO over 1 h increases rapidly first from 45.30 % to 81.42 %, and then slightly drop to 77.79 %. The NO photodegradation efficiencies of the GQDs/BOC samples are 1.51–1.71 times better than BiOCl. This is because the addition of GQDs can increase the specific surface area of catalyst, enhance absorption capacity of visible lights, and accelerates the separation of photogenerated carriers. Specifically, the degradation percentages of GQDs10/BOC and GQDs20/BOC are both lower than that of GQDs30/BOC, which may be attributed to insufficient GQDs deposited on the BiOCl surface. On the contrary, the catalytic activity of GQDs40/BOC decreases compared to that of GQDs30/BOC. This is because excessive GQDs blocks the absorption of visible lights (see Fig. 3a) (Xian et al., 2019).

Fig. S7 illustrates NO, NO2, and HNO2 concentrations at the outlet for GQDs30/BOC catalysts over time, where
C
N
out
represents the sum of NO, NO2, and HNO2 concentrations at the outlet. The
C
N
out
is less than the concentration of NO at the inlet, which means that NO is converted other nitrogen-containing substances deposited on the surface of catalyst in addition to NO2 and HNO2 in the gas phase. The transformation of nitrogen (N) is important to understanding the mechanisms behind the photocatalysis of NO. The IC result shows that
N O
3
-
and
N O
2
-
ions are formed on the catalyst surface. Therefore, NO is transformed into NO2(g), HNO2(g), HNO2(ads) and HNO3(ads) products by photocatalysis using GQDs30/BOC composites.

Fig. 4b shows the DeNOx indices of different samples. Positive DeNOx index is desirable because it indicates low secondary pollution to the environment during NO photocatalytic degradation. All GQDs/BOC composites outperform pure BiOCl in terms of DeNOx index, and that of GQDs30/BOC is the greatest. Specifically, the DeNOx index rises from 0.25 of pure BiOCl to 0.55 of GQDs30/BOC. However, excessive GQDs negatively affects the DeNOx index. These results indicate that an appropriate amount of GQDs is important to the environmental friendliness of the composite photocatalyst.

Fig. 4f displays the photocatalytic NO conversion of four consecutive cycles using GQDs30/BOC photocatalyst. After four cycles, the GQDs30/BOC catalyst shows a slight decrease in activity compared to the fresh one, but a simple heating regeneration allows it to maintain 73 % of fresh catalytic activity. This indicates that the prepared catalyst possesses good stability for the degradation of NO.

We compared our result in Table S3 with other reports on photocatalytic NO degradation. Our catalyst outweighs its competitors in terms of NO conversion rate. When compared to the two works that used 50 mg of catalyst, our catalyst loading is 1.6 times greater than their loadings; however, we achieve much higher NO conversion efficiency at an inlet NO concentration approximately 53 times higher than the counterparts in these two works. When compared to other reports using larger amounts of catalysts, our advantage becomes even more apparent, because we achieved the highest conversion rate, while using the least amount of catalyst to degrade the highest NO concentration.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanisms behind photocatalytic degradation of NO
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 4d and e illustrate the ESR signals of DMPO-O2•− and DMPO- OH•. Fig. 4d shows no ESR signal in the dark environment. Instead, under illumination, there are four strong DMPO-O2•− peaks. This indicates the production of O2•− in the photocatalytic process. Conversely, Fig. 4e shows no obvious ESR signals of OH• are detected in both dark and light, indicating that OH• cannot be generated during the photocatalysis using GQDs30/BOC.

Additionally, the main active substances during photocatalytic reactions with NO on GQDs30/BOC are identified by scavenging tests. K2Cr2O7 and KI were chosen as scavengers for e− and h+ , respectively. As shown in Fig. 4c, taking GQDs30/BOC as an example, the catalytic ability of the catalyst in photocatalytic NO degradation is greatly reduced or even lost in the presence of KI and K2Cr2O7. When H2O is removed from the mixture, the degradation rate of NO is slightly dropped from 81.42 % to 72.49 %. This indicates the OH• plays a negligible role in NO photocatalysis, which is consistent with ESR results. The slight reduction in degradation efficiency caused by removing moisture is because H2O itself participates in the reactions between photogenerated electrons and NO to generate NO2, NO3 – and HNO2 (Shi et al., 2019; Zhu et al., 2019). When O2 is removed from the gas mixture, however, the NO degradation efficiency dramatically decreases from 81.42 % to 52.83 %. These findings demonstrate that O2•− plays an important part in NO conversion. These results indicate that e− , h+ and
·
O
2
-
are the main active substances in the degradation of NO by GQDs30/BOC, while the role of ⋅OH is slight.

DFT calculation elucidates the adsorption sites of GQDs and the transfer of charges between GQDs and BiOCl. After the structure of GQDs/BOC is optimized, the adsorption energy of each position is calculated. The DFT results show that the adsorption energy is ranked in an ascending order of Position 3 (−3.88 eV) < Position 2 (−2.16 eV) < Position 1 (−2.08 eV). Moreover, the distances between the C and O atoms at Positions 3, 2 and 1 are 1.484 Å, 2.717 Å, and 2.840 Å, respectively. Such information indicates that the adsorption between BiOCl and GQDs is the strongest at Position 3 (see Fig. S2). Therefore, Position 3 is selected for subsequent calculations.

Fig. 5 a and d present the main and top views of the charge density difference. The blue areas represent the decrease of electron density, and yellow areas represent electrons accumulation. The color difference suggests that the electron density decreases around GQDs and electrons gather near BiOCl. In addition, Bader charge calculation shows that 0.0346 electrons transfer from GQDs to the BiOCl surface. This electron transfer builds up an electric field directing from GQDs to BiOCl. Upon visible-light irradiation on GQDs/BOC composites, the photoexcited electrons on the BiOCl surface migrate to GQDs because of the built-in electric field.

Fig. 5b and c present the PDOS of the pure BiOCl. The valence band maximums (VBM) consist of Cl 3p, O 2p and a few Bi 6 s orbitals, and the conduction band minimums (CBM) consist of Bi 6p and O 2p orbitals. However, with the presence of GQDs, the VBM consist of the newly introduced C 2p orbitals. The new p-orbital, highlighted in the blue area in Fig. 5e, transits the components of conduction band minimum (CBM) from predominant Bi 6p and O 2p orbitals for BiOCl to predominant C 2p orbitals for GQDs/BOC. The presence of C 2p orbital shifts the conduction band to the left, greatly reducing the band gap. This is consistent with the MS measurements and UV–Vis DRS measurements.

The band gap of a crystal is related to its primary exposed faces (Qi et al., 2017). Based on the XRD results in Fig. 1a, pure BiOCl has a primary exposed face of (101), but it shifts from (101) of pure phase to (110) of GQDs/BOC composites after adding GQDs. The DFT calculations of the band gaps for BiOCl with exposed (101) face, BiOCl with exposed (110) face, and GQDs/BOC (110) composites can help determine the cause for the change in band gap before and after adding GQDs. Fig. 5g–i display that the band gap of BiOCl with exposed (110) face (i.e., 3.03 eV) is wider than that of BiOCl with exposed (101) face (i.e., 2.32 eV). However, the band gap of GQDs/BOC (110) composites reduces to 1.93 eV after the addition of GQDs. This suggests that the smaller band gap of the GQDs/BOC composites is caused by the doping GQDs, rather than the alteration in exposed face.

The mechanisms behind the photocatalytic degradation NO by GQDs30/BOC composites can then be explored by the analysis of re-dox capability and DFT calculations. The CB potential of the composite is −0.64 V (vs. NHE), which is lower than O2/O2•− (-0.33 V vs. NHE) (Fan et al., 2022). This indicates that photoelectrons can reduce O2 to O2•− following Eq. (2). Then NO react with the O2•− to produce NO2 − and NO3 −, following Eq. (3) and Eq. (4) (Sugrañez et al., 2015; Yang et al., 2021), respectively.

Furthermore, the VB potential of GQDs30/BOC is 1.90 V vs. NHE according to Section 3.3.1, suggesting that valence bands are unable to oxidize OH– (OH–/OH• 1.99 V vs. NHE) (Hu et al., 2022) or H2O (H2O/OH• 2.30 V vs. NHE) into OH• (Shi et al., 2019; Zhang et al., 2013). This echoes the findings of ESR tests and sacrificial agent. Nevertheless, the photogenerated holes can directly oxidize the NO adsorbate on the catalyst into NO2 (NO/NO2, 1.03 V vs. NHE) following Eq. (5), NO3 – (NO/NO3 –, 0.94 V vs. NHE) following Eq. (7), or HNO2 (NO/HNO2, 0.99 V vs. NHE) following Eq. (8) (Zhu et al., 2019). The NO2 continues to react with the O2•− to form NO3 – following Eq. (6) (Yang et al., 2021). Therefore, Eqs. (1) to (8) explain the mechanisms behind the efficient removal of NO using GQDs30/BOC photocatalyst, which are then visualized in Fig. 6 .

The GQDs/BOC photocatalysts responsive to visible lights are prepared by a semi-solvothermal method. The XRD patterns and HR-TEM images show that the incorporation of GQDs affects the growth of BiOCl crystal surfaces. The characterization of XPS, EDS and HR-TEM support the GQDs loading onto BiOCl surfaces. The BET results show that the specific surface area and pore width of the GQDs30/BOC composites are improved compared with pure BiOCl. Compared to pure BiOCl, the GQDs30/BOC composites increase the degradation efficiency of NO from 45.30 % to 81.42 %, and the DeNOx index from 0.25 to 0.55. The ESR characterizations and scavenging tests show that O2•− plays a major role in the photocatalytic degradation of NO. The combination of UV–Vis DRS measurement, MS test, and DFT calculations indicates that GQDs in GQDs/BOC composites act as photoelectron acceptors under illumination, and promote the separation of photogenerated carriers. The cycle experiments prove that the prepared catalysts are stable and reusable.
